subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Social Influence and Persuasion
stimulus:
Mobile Gamer Central (MGC) is preparing to launch a new game app. The advertisements for this new game
are brightly colored, have lively music, and feature celebrities playing the game. To generate interest in the
game, MGC pays to have these advertisements pop up multiple times while people are using other apps on their
phone. The marketing director is pleased with the advertising campaign and thinks the game is really fun to
play.

parts.prompt: Explain how Peripheral route to persuasion relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description:
Response must indicate that the advertisements utilize factors other than the quality of the  game (e.g., bright colors, lively music, or celebrities shown in the ads) to persuade  consumers. 

OR 

Response must indicate that the person exposed to the advertisement is persuaded by  factors other than quality of the game (e.g., bright colors, lively music, or celebrities shown  in the ads).

required_elements: The people liked the game because of who the celebrities were who were playing it in the ads. Because they included lots of bright colors and music in their commercials, it is clear that MGC wanted to employ the peripheral route to persuasion in their advertising.
alternatives.correct_example: The people liked the game because of who the celebrities were who were playing it in the ads. Because they included lots of bright colors and music in their commercials, it is clear that MGC wanted to employ the peripheral route to persuasion in their advertising.

required_elements: MGC tried to get people to buy the game for reasons other than that the game is well- designed.
alternatives.correct_example: MGC tried to get people to buy the game for reasons other than that the game is well- designed.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The peripheral route to persuasion involves being persuaded by superficial cues (attractiveness, emotion, celebrity endorsement, music, colors) rather than by the logical quality of the argument or the product itself. The response must identify one of these peripheral cues in the scenario and explain how it persuades. Responses that say customers like the game for its actual gameplay quality (a central route cue) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: People liked the game because of the celebrities featured in the ads — they were persuaded by the peripheral cue of celebrity appeal rather than by evaluating the game's actual quality.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies celebrity endorsement as a peripheral (non-quality) cue used to persuade consumers.

sample_responses.response: MGC used bright colors and lively music in their ads — peripheral cues designed to create a positive emotional association with the game rather than highlighting any actual game features.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — identifies multiple peripheral cues (colors, music) that bypass logical evaluation of the product.

sample_responses.response: The customers like the game because it is easy to play.
sample_responses.commentary: Ease of play is a characteristic of the product itself — a central route consideration — not a peripheral cue.

parts.prompt: Explain how False consensus effect relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that, because an individual related to the scenario really likes the  game/advertising, they overestimate the extent to which other people will like the  game/advertising.

required_elements: The marketing director thinks everybody will like the game as much as he does. The marketing director thinks more people will buy the game than actually do because he enjoys it so much. A consumer really likes the pop-up ads and believes that most other people will like them as well.
alternatives.correct_example: The marketing director thinks everybody will like the game as much as he does. The marketing director thinks more people will buy the game than actually do because he enjoys it so much. A consumer really likes the pop-up ads and believes that most other people will like them as well.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The false consensus effect is the tendency to overestimate how widely one's own attitudes, beliefs, or preferences are shared by others. The response must show an individual (the marketing director or a consumer) projecting their own liking of the game/ads onto others. Responses that describe groupthink or conformity (people suppressing opinions due to social pressure) do not earn the point. Responses that show consumers being influenced by what they believe others think (rather than projecting their own views) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Because the marketing director really enjoys the game, he overestimates how many other people will also like it and buy it — this is the false consensus effect.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly shows the marketing director projecting his own positive experience onto the general population.

sample_responses.response: A consumer really likes the pop-up ads and assumes that most other people find them appealing as well.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — consumer projects their own positive attitude toward the ads onto others, overestimating consensus.

sample_responses.response: The marketing director's opinion is so strong that the other people on the team keep their opinions to themselves.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes groupthink or conformity (others suppressing disagreement), not the false consensus effect (overestimating agreement).

parts.prompt: Explain how Mere-exposure effect relates to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that repeated exposure(s) to the game/advertisement will increase  the liking/enjoyment of the game/advertisement.

required_elements: The more often people see the ads pop up on their phone, the more they will like the game. People liked the advertisement the second time they saw it more than the first time. The more times people play the game, the more likely they are to buy it.
alternatives.correct_example: The more often people see the ads pop up on their phone, the more they will like the game. People liked the advertisement the second time they saw it more than the first time. The more times people play the game, the more likely they are to buy it.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The mere-exposure effect states that simply being exposed to a stimulus repeatedly increases liking for it. The response must show repeated exposure to the game or advertisement increasing liking or purchase likelihood. Responses that say people enjoy the game simply by playing it (without emphasizing the repetition component) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The more often people see the pop-up ads on their phone, the more they will like the game — repeated exposure increases liking through the mere-exposure effect.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies repeated exposure (multiple pop-ups) as the mechanism increasing liking for the game.

sample_responses.response: People liked the advertisement the second time they saw it more than the first time because of the mere-exposure effect.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — shows liking increasing with a second exposure, a direct application of the mere-exposure effect.

sample_responses.response: By playing the game, the people enjoy it and want to download it.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes enjoyment from a single experience with the game rather than from repeated exposure. The mere-exposure effect requires the repetition component.

parts.prompt:
After a few weeks with moderate success, MGC's marketing director decides to test the most effective ways to increase sales of the game. Marketing researchers recruit 100 people to play the new game. They randomly assign half of the people to observe someone demonstrating how the game is played and assign the other half of the people to play the video game themselves. Participants rate how likely they are to buy the game on a scale of 1 (not likely) through 10 (very likely). Results are presented in the table.

| | Observed Game Demonstration (n = 50) | Personally Played Game (n = 50) | p value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean | 6.31 | 2.04 | < 0.001 |
| Standard Deviation | 1.25 | 0.80 | |

Identify the operational definition of the dependent variable in the study.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the operational definition of the dependent variable is the  rating/score/number on the scale used in the study.

required_elements: The rating of the game on a scale of 1–10 is the dependent variable. The score of the survey indicating how likely the participant is to buy the game is the operational definition of the dependent variable.
alternatives.correct_example: The rating of the game on a scale of 1–10 is the dependent variable. The score of the survey indicating how likely the participant is to buy the game is the operational definition of the dependent variable.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The operational definition must specify the scale/rating used to measure the DV. Responses that identify the group assignment (observed vs. played), whether people will buy the game as a yes/no outcome, or simply 'how much people like the game' without referencing the rating scale do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The operational definition of the dependent variable is the participant's rating on the 1-to-10 scale of how likely they are to buy the game.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the scale score as the operational definition of the DV (likelihood to purchase).

sample_responses.response: The score on the survey indicating how likely the participant is to buy the game is the operational definition of the dependent variable.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — references the survey score as the measurement instrument for the DV.

sample_responses.response: The operational definition of the DV is whether people will buy the game.
sample_responses.commentary: This is a yes/no outcome, not the operational definition. The study measures likelihood on a scale, not a binary purchase decision.

sample_responses.response: The operational definition of the dependent variable is which group the people were in.
sample_responses.commentary: Group assignment is the independent variable (observed vs. played), not the dependent variable.

parts.prompt:
After a few weeks with moderate success, MGC's marketing director decides to test the most effective ways to increase sales of the game. Marketing researchers recruit 100 people to play the new game. They randomly assign half of the people to observe someone demonstrating how the game is played and assign the other half of the people to play the video game themselves. Participants rate how likely they are to buy the game on a scale of 1 (not likely) through 10 (very likely). Results are presented in the table.

| | Observed Game Demonstration (n = 50) | Personally Played Game (n = 50) | p value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean | 6.31 | 2.04 | < 0.001 |
| Standard Deviation | 1.25 | 0.80 | |

Explain what the difference between the standard deviations in the study indicates.

scoring_points.description:
Response must indicate that the "personally played game" group ratings are more  similar/less varied. 

OR 

Response must indicate that the "observed game demonstration" group ratings differ  more/varied more.

required_elements: The personally played game group had responses that were more alike because their standard deviation is closer to zero. The ratings in the group who observed the game were more spread out than the group who played the game.
alternatives.correct_example: The personally played game group had responses that were more alike because their standard deviation is closer to zero. The ratings in the group who observed the game were more spread out than the group who played the game.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must interpret the difference in variability between the two groups: the personally played group (SD = 0.80) had more consistent/less varied ratings than the observed group (SD = 1.25). Either framing earns the point. Responses that describe the difference in means (higher vs. lower scores) rather than the difference in variability do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The personally played game group had ratings that were more consistent with each other because their standard deviation (0.80) is closer to zero than the observed group's (1.25).
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly interprets the smaller SD as indicating less variability in the personally played group's ratings.

sample_responses.response: The ratings in the group who observed the game were more spread out than those who played the game, as shown by the higher standard deviation of 1.25 versus 0.80.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — identifies the observed group as having more variable ratings, correctly reading the SD comparison.

sample_responses.response: The observed game group had higher scores than the personally played game group.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the difference in means, not the difference in variability. Standard deviation measures spread, not central tendency.

parts.prompt:
After a few weeks with moderate success, MGC's marketing director decides to test the most effective ways to increase sales of the game. Marketing researchers recruit 100 people to play the new game. They randomly assign half of the people to observe someone demonstrating how the game is played and assign the other half of the people to play the video game themselves. Participants rate how likely they are to buy the game on a scale of 1 (not likely) through 10 (very likely). Results are presented in the table.

| | Observed Game Demonstration (n = 50) | Personally Played Game (n = 50) | p value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean | 6.31 | 2.04 | < 0.001 |
| Standard Deviation | 1.25 | 0.80 | |

Explain why random assignment is necessary for determining cause and effect in the study.

scoring_points.description: Responses must indicate that random assignment is necessary because it would help  eliminate or reduce the impact of confounding variables related to individual differences.

required_elements: The researchers used random assignment to make sure individual differences are controlled for. The researchers used random assignment to make sure differences between participants are more likely to be spread out evenly between the two groups. The researchers used random assignment to make sure gender wasn't the reason why people wanted to buy the app.
alternatives.correct_example: The researchers used random assignment to make sure individual differences are controlled for. The researchers used random assignment to make sure differences between participants are more likely to be spread out evenly between the two groups. The researchers used random assignment to make sure gender wasn't the reason why people wanted to buy the app.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must explain that random assignment controls for individual differences (confounding variables) by distributing them evenly across both groups. Responses that describe random selection (choosing participants) rather than random assignment (distributing to conditions) do not earn the point. Stating only that random assignment is needed for a study to be an experiment, without explaining why, does not earn the point. Vague references to 'eliminating bias' without specifying the mechanism (equating groups on individual differences) do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Random assignment ensures that individual differences (such as prior gaming experience) are distributed evenly between the two groups, so those differences cannot explain any difference in ratings.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the function of random assignment — equating groups on potential confounding individual differences.

sample_responses.response: The researchers used random assignment to make sure that differences between participants (like gender or age) are more likely to be spread out evenly between the two groups.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — identifies specific individual difference variables (gender, age) that random assignment controls for.

sample_responses.response: They must get a random group of people to participate in order to establish cause and effect.
sample_responses.commentary: Confuses random selection (sampling) with random assignment (condition allocation). Random selection determines who participates; random assignment determines which condition they are in.

sample_responses.response: Random assignment will allow the MGC researchers to eliminate bias and confounding variables.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague. Does not explain the mechanism — that random assignment controls for individual differences by distributing them across groups.

parts.prompt:
After a few weeks with moderate success, MGC's marketing director decides to test the most effective ways to increase sales of the game. Marketing researchers recruit 100 people to play the new game. They randomly assign half of the people to observe someone demonstrating how the game is played and assign the other half of the people to play the video game themselves. Participants rate how likely they are to buy the game on a scale of 1 (not likely) through 10 (very likely). Results are presented in the table.

| | Observed Game Demonstration (n = 50) | Personally Played Game (n = 50) | p value |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mean | 6.31 | 2.04 | < 0.001 |
| Standard Deviation | 1.25 | 0.80 | |

Explain what the p value allows MGC to conclude about the study.

scoring_points.description:
Response must indicate that the p value allows MGC to conclude that the IV (observing vs.  playing the game) caused a change in the DV (ratings). 

OR 

Response must indicate that the p value allows MGC to conclude that the results of the  study or difference between the groups' ratings were significant. 

OR 

Response must indicate that the p value allows MGC to conclude that the difference  between the groups' ratings was not (or not likely) due to chance.

required_elements: The p value allows them to conclude that the people who observed the game demo were significantly more likely to say they would buy the app. The p value says that the groups' ratings were different enough to be statistically significant. The p value says that the difference in the group's ratings about the game is not likely due to chance. The p value allows MGC researchers to reject the null hypothesis.
alternatives.correct_example: The p value allows them to conclude that the people who observed the game demo were significantly more likely to say they would buy the app. The p value says that the groups' ratings were different enough to be statistically significant. The p value says that the difference in the group's ratings about the game is not likely due to chance. The p value allows MGC researchers to reject the null hypothesis.

scoring_points.official_rubric: A p value of <0.001 allows the researchers to conclude the results are statistically significant — the difference between groups is unlikely to be due to chance. Three equivalent acceptable framings: (1) the IV caused a change in the DV, (2) the difference was statistically significant, (3) the result is not likely due to chance. Responses that describe the p value as confirming accuracy or that misinterpret significance as participant-level enthusiasm do not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The p value of <0.001 allows MGC to conclude that the difference in ratings between the two groups is statistically significant and not likely due to chance.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly interprets the p value as indicating the result is not due to chance — a standard valid interpretation.

sample_responses.response: The p value allows MGC to conclude that observing the game demonstration caused higher purchase likelihood ratings — the IV caused a change in the DV.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable — interprets the p value as supporting a causal conclusion (IV caused DV change).

sample_responses.response: The p value shows that the results are accurate.
sample_responses.commentary: Conflates statistical significance with accuracy or reliability of measurement. The p value indicates significance, not accuracy.

sample_responses.response: The p value shows that the people who played the game found the experience to be significant.
sample_responses.commentary: Misapplies 'significant' to describe the participants' subjective experience rather than the statistical concept of significance.
